food_subgroup: Rice bowl dish with beef and dairy topping

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'beef bowl' class (FOODON_00002809) which is the closest parent; no specific cheese gyudon class exists in FoodOn.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKW (beef and rice dish) is the closest defensible parent category; no specific gyudon or cheese gyudon code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept exists for gyudon or cheese gyudon. No suitable parent code identified without risk of fabrication.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Melted Cheese Gyudon is approximately 400 g, comprising a bowl of steamed white rice topped with simmered beef and onion in a sweet-savory sauce, finished with a generous layer of melted processed cheese.

nutrient_source.title: Beef bowl with cheese — composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central entries: Beef, short loin, top loin steak (SR Legacy #13938); White rice, cooked (SR Legacy #20444); Cheese, processed American (SR Legacy #01042); Onion, cooked (SR Legacy #11284); and standard gyudon sauce components
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for cheese gyudon exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA Branded Foods, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted-ingredient composition: approximately 45% cooked white rice, 25% simmered beef (thin-sliced), 15% melted processed cheese, 10% onion, and 5% sauce (soy sauce, mirin, dashi). All component values sourced from USDA SR Legacy. Energy cross-checked using Atwater factors.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for white rice (cooked, Japanese-style, GI ~72)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for cheese gyudon. The GI of 72 is assigned based on the dominant glycemic driver being Japanese-style cooked white rice, which has a well-established GI of approximately 72 (high) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283). The cheese and beef components contribute negligible glycemic load. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (72 × 79.2 g available carbohydrate per 400 g serving) / 100 = 57.0.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce, dashi broth, and processed cheese
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its estimated sodium content of approximately 380 mg per 100 g (approximately 1,520 mg per standard 400 g serving), which represents a substantial proportion of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index driven by white rice base and moderate sugar content from sweet soy-mirin sauce
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should exercise caution with this dish, as the white rice base confers a high GI of approximately 72 and a glycemic load of approximately 57 per serving, which may cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; portion reduction and substitution with lower-GI grains are advisable.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from processed cheese and beef, combined with high sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish infrequently, as the combination of saturated fat (approximately 3.1 g per 100 g, primarily from processed cheese and beef) and high sodium content may adversely affect blood pressure and lipid profiles.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Melted Cheese Gyudon is a Japanese beef rice bowl dish (gyudon) topped with a generous layer of melted processed cheese, popularised in Japan by the fast-food chain Sukiya. The base consists of steamed Japanese short-grain white rice, over which thinly sliced beef and onion are simmered in a sweet-savory sauce made from dashi broth, soy sauce, mirin, and sugar. The dish is finished by placing processed cheese slices on top of the hot beef mixture and allowing them to melt into a creamy, rich layer. The result is a combination of umami-rich beef, subtly sweet sauce, and the salty, creamy flavour of melted cheese. Texturally, the dish offers soft rice, tender beef, and a smooth, slightly stretchy cheese topping. Nutritionally, it is a calorie-dense, high-sodium, high-GI meal providing moderate protein from beef and cheese, significant carbohydrate from white rice, and saturated fat from the cheese component. It is not suitable for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease without significant portion control or dietary modification. In Singapore, it is served at Sukiya outlets and is marketed as a popular Japanese comfort food.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for cheese gyudon was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA Branded Foods, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted-ingredient composition using USDA SR Legacy component data. The GI is estimated from the dominant white rice component using the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level USDA data is high quality and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted-ingredient composition (approximately 45% cooked white rice, 25% simmered thin-sliced beef, 15% melted processed cheese, 10% cooked onion, 5% sauce) using USDA SR Legacy data. No exact branded or restaurant-specific nutritional panel was available from an approved source. 2. The food_group field has been corrected from the input value of 'Dairy and eggs' to 'Mixed', as this is a composite rice bowl dish with beef, dairy, and grain components. 3. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on the white rice base; no direct GI measurement for cheese gyudon exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 4. Glycemic load per serving = (72 × 79.2) / 100 = 57.0, where 79.2 g is the estimated available carbohydrate in a 400 g serving (19.8 g per 100 g × 4). 5. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, and all B-vitamins and micronutrients are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data and are approximate. 6. The dish is not confirmed halal at Sukiya Singapore; halal status should be verified with the restaurant directly. 7. Cuisine tags from the input ('Japanese Curry', 'Grilled Chicken Skewers') are irrelevant to this dish and have been disregarded. 8. All -1 sentinel values have been replaced with estimated numeric values from USDA SR Legacy component data; no -1 values remain in per_100g.
